title.zh: 第七回 問罪燈前 昭明垂淚
title.en: Chapter 7: Demanding an Accounting by Lamplight; Zhao Ming Holds Back His Tears

en: An accounting by lamplight — the calculus is without flaw
Silence becomes a country no one can leave

passages.zh: 卻說萬靈坡一役既畢，西路已定，吳烈收兵還營。璟同之喪事，昭明已命副將料理，棺木就地以青松覆蓋，暫停于坡下草廬。吳烈歸帳，默坐燈前，三日不發一語。帳中諸將輪番入稟軍情，皆悄聲報完，躡足而退，無人敢高聲。雲素華伏於帳角錄事，筆端數度停頓，硯中之墨漸幹而不知，惟見吳烈側臉如雕，燈光在其顴骨之上搖曳，照出一道深而無底的暗影。
passages.en: After the battle at Ten-Thousand-Spirit Slope was done and the western approach secured, Wu Lie drew in his troops and returned to camp. The funeral arrangements for Jing Tong had already been delegated by Zhao Ming to the deputy commanders; the coffin was laid below the slope and covered with green pine boughs in a temporary grass shelter. Wu Lie returned to his tent and sat in silence before the lamp for three days without speaking a word. The various generals entered in turn to report on military affairs, each delivering his report in a low voice before tiptoeing away — none dared to raise their voice. Yun Suhua crouched at the corner of the tent recording events; several times her brush paused, and the ink in the inkstone dried without her noticing. She could only watch Wu Lie's profile, carved and still as stone, the lamplight swaying on his cheekbones, casting a shadow deep and without a bottom.

passages.zh: 第四日晨，一名後營小校奉命往昭明帳中問取西路圖冊，取圖歸途，與數名同袍相遇，語聲稍高，談及萬靈坡佈陣之事，有言曰：「昭明先生早料西路不保，只不過未曾知會主公罷了。」此語傳入一旁巡查之親兵耳中，親兵默然，轉身入帳，低聲告知吳烈。吳烈聽罷，閉目片刻，睜眼時面色已變，既非怒，亦非悲，乃是一種前所未有的冷靜，冷如磨刀之水，無聲而利。他只道：「傳昭明。」
passages.en: On the morning of the fourth day, a junior officer from the rear camp was sent to Zhao Ming's tent to retrieve the western-approach maps. On his return with the maps, he encountered several comrades, and their voices rose a little as they discussed the deployment at Ten-Thousand-Spirit Slope. One of them said: 'Master Zhao Ming had long calculated that the western approach would not hold — only he had not thought to inform the lord.' These words reached the ears of a personal guard passing by on patrol. The guard said nothing. He turned and entered the tent, and in a low voice relayed what he had heard to Wu Lie. Wu Lie listened, then closed his eyes for a moment. When he opened them, his complexion had changed — it was neither rage nor grief but rather a quality of calm he had never shown before, cold as the water used to whet a blade, silent and keen. He said only: 'Summon Zhao Ming.'

en: Three days without speech — the lamp did not go out
A single sentence, blade-like, cuts into the tent
Zhao Ming knows the weight of this moment
Winter snow falls to the ground and makes no sound

passages.zh: 昭明入帳，見吳烈獨坐燈前，手中無物，案上亦空，唯那一盞油燈，火焰細而穩，如審者之目。昭明長揖不語，吳烈亦不命其起，兩人如此對峙片刻。吳烈終開口，聲音平緩如問路之人，曰：「你早知西路會失？」昭明挺直了腰，答曰：「是。」吳烈再問：「你知璟同若守西路，難以生還？」昭明不動聲色，答曰：「以西路守軍之力，抵敵先鋒半個時辰，已是昭明所能推算之極限。如實回稟：弟子早知會失，亦早知守者難歸。」
passages.en: Zhao Ming entered the tent to find Wu Lie seated alone before the lamp, his hands holding nothing, the table bare, only that single oil-lamp burning — its flame thin and steady, like the eye of an examiner. Zhao Ming bowed deeply and said nothing. Wu Lie did not bid him rise, and the two men held this posture facing each other for a moment. At last Wu Lie spoke. His voice was even, like a man asking directions on a road: 'You knew from the start that the western approach would fall?' Zhao Ming straightened his spine and answered: 'Yes.' Wu Lie asked again: 'You knew that if Jing Tong held the western approach, he would not likely return alive?' Zhao Ming did not change his expression. He answered: 'For the forces assigned to hold the western approach to resist the enemy vanguard for half an hour — that was the outermost limit of what this disciple could calculate as possible. To report honestly: I had known from the beginning that it would fall, and had known from the beginning that the one who held it would not come back.'

passages.zh: 帳中靜了許久，油燈芯子輕爆一聲，火焰跳了一下，復歸原形。昭明繼而言道：「昭明以西路之一人，換東路萬餘人之生路，換中路主陣之不潰，此乃戰場算術，無可更改。若主公事前知曉，必不忍壯士就死，則東路將潰，中路亦潰，兩萬人不得歸；璟同或死或俘，主公亦身陷重圍，九爐大局從此易主。彼時而論，一人之死，何輕？萬人之死，何重？」吳烈聽罷，低頭，以指節叩案一聲，道：「你說得對。」
passages.en: The tent was silent for a long time. The lamp-wick crackled softly once; the flame leapt and then settled back to its original shape. Zhao Ming continued: 'By sacrificing one man on the western approach, this counselor won the path of life for more than ten thousand men on the eastern flank, and prevented the collapse of the central formation. This is the arithmetic of the battlefield; it cannot be altered. Had my lord known in advance, he would not have been able to bear sending a brave warrior to his death — the eastern flank would have broken, the center would have broken, twenty thousand men would not have returned. Jing Tong might have died or been captured regardless, and my lord too would have been encircled and overwhelmed, and the great situation of the Nine Furnaces would have changed hands from that hour. Weighed against that outcome — is one death light? Are ten thousand deaths heavy?' Wu Lie heard him out, bowed his head, and struck the table once with his knuckle. He said: 'You are right.'

passages.zh: 然吳烈未曾停歇，抬起眼，目光直視昭明，徐徐又道：「那萬餘人，如今可有一人，知曉璟同之名？」此語一出，昭明面色微變，沉默如壁。吳烈不等他答，亦不再追問，只轉目望向燈焰，復不言語。一室之中，唯有燈光搖曳，映出兩個人的影子，一靜一顫，皆如墨痕。昭明立於原地，忽覺目中有物，強壓之，壓之不住，低首時，一滴落於靴前青石地板，無聲碎散，如那一場算術中從未被書寫的名字。
passages.en: Yet Wu Lie did not stop. He raised his eyes and looked directly at Zhao Ming, speaking slowly once more: 'Among those ten thousand men — does even one of them know Jing Tong's name?' At these words Zhao Ming's complexion shifted almost imperceptibly. He was silent as a wall. Wu Lie did not wait for an answer, nor did he press the question further; he simply turned his gaze back to the lamp's flame and said nothing more. In the room there was only the lamp swaying, casting the shadows of two men — one still, one trembling — both like traces of ink. Zhao Ming stood in place, and suddenly felt something rise in his eyes. He pressed it down; it would not be pressed. When he bowed his head, one drop fell onto the green stone floor before his boots, scattering soundlessly — like a name that had never been written into that piece of battlefield arithmetic.

redComment.anchor: 可有一人，知曉璟同之名
redComment.zh: 此一句乃全書之錐心之語。算術能算存亡，卻算不得姓名；昭明之術既精且孤，於此刻悉數崩解。批者擱筆，久不能續。
redComment.en: This sentence is the line that pierces to the heart of the entire work. Arithmetic can calculate survival and extinction, but it cannot calculate a name. Zhao Ming's craft, precise and solitary, collapses entirely at this moment. The commentator sets down the brush and cannot continue for a long while.

en: The road home for ten thousand men was opened by one man
The abacus flawless — and yet it laughs at nothing but dust
Ten thousand names the ledger cannot finish writing
Only these two words — Jing Tong — are absent from the page

passages.zh: 事後，吳烈既不解除昭明之職，亦不革除其參議之權，更不逐之出帳。他只是此後不再與昭明同席用膳，不再獨召昭明夜議，凡有戰事，傳令之人來去皆是副將，昭明之謀略照舊施行，吳烈之令照舊採納，然二人之間多出一片空地，空地之上無有風雨，無有日光，無有話語，唯有那一個問句，懸於其上，不落不散。昭明心知此乃重罰，重於奪官，重於廷杖，重於一切可名狀之懲處，因此懲處乃以沉默為刃，深入而無形。
passages.en: Afterward, Wu Lie neither relieved Zhao Ming of his post nor stripped him of his advisory authority, nor drove him from the tent. It was simply that from then onward Wu Lie no longer shared a table with Zhao Ming at meals, no longer summoned him alone for night consultations; in all military matters it was a deputy commander who carried orders back and forth. Zhao Ming's stratagems were implemented as before; Wu Lie's commands continued to draw upon them. Yet between the two men a vacant ground had opened — a ground without wind or rain, without sunlight, without words — only that one question, hanging suspended above it, neither falling nor dispersing. Zhao Ming understood that this was a heavy punishment: heavier than stripping rank, heavier than the court rod, heavier than any penalty that could be named, because this punishment used silence as its blade, cutting deep and leaving no visible wound.

redComment.zh: 吳烈之沉默，非一人之沉默，乃一國之沉默。昭明既不能辯，亦不能逃，更不能贖，此後行走九爐，皆帶此問句於身，如枷如影。
redComment.en: Wu Lie's silence is not the silence of one man but the silence of a country. Zhao Ming can neither argue, nor escape, nor atone. From this point onward he walks through the Nine Furnaces carrying that question on his person — like a cangue, like a shadow.

en: No dismissal, no exile, no punishment pronounced
Only that from this day forward each moves through the world alone
Two lamps in the tent that once shared a single flame
Hereafter each illuminates its own half-wall of ice

passages.zh: 是月下旬，霧胤遣使持白旗入吳烈大營。使者言，霧胤亦傷亡慘重，糧草不繼，天命璽雖在其手，然玄璽不能果腹，玄璽不能行軍，霧胤願暫罷干戈，兩軍各守現有疆界，璽之歸屬，來日再議。吳烈召昭明及諸將議事，帳中意見各異，主戰者有之，主和者有之。昭明陳言：「乘勝追擊，固可一鼓；然吾軍亦疲，璟同既歿，西路空虛，此時強攻，勝算不足六分，若再一折，九爐大局恐難挽回。臣以為，暫息可也。」吳烈聽罷，頷首，下令與霧胤暫時休戰，立界石為憑。
passages.en: In the latter part of that month, Wu Yin sent an emissary bearing a white banner into Wu Lie's main camp. The emissary reported that Wu Yin too had suffered heavy casualties and could no longer sustain his supply lines. The Jade Seal of Heaven's Mandate was in his hands, but the Seal could not fill an empty belly and could not lead troops on the march; Wu Yin was willing to lay down arms for the time being, each army to hold its present territory, and the question of the Seal's ultimate disposition to be settled another day. Wu Lie summoned Zhao Ming and the assembled generals to deliberate in the tent. Opinion in the tent was divided: some urged pressing the attack while victory was momentum, others urged acceptance of the truce. Zhao Ming spoke: 'To strike while the momentum is ours — yes, we could do it with a single drum-beat. Yet our army is also exhausted; with Jing Tong gone, the western approach is hollow; to assault now, our odds of success do not reach six in ten. If we suffer another setback, the great situation of the Nine Furnaces may be beyond recovery. This counselor holds that a temporary halt is acceptable.' Wu Lie heard him out, inclined his head, and issued the order to accept a temporary ceasefire with Wu Yin, marking the boundary with standing stones.

passages.zh: 休戰文書既定，使者去後，諸將各散。帳中唯餘吳烈與雲素華。素華收起筆硯，起身欲退，吳烈忽曰：「素華，你可知世界之西邊，盡頭是何所在？」素華愕然，回首見吳烈正目視帳頂，神情遙遠，如在問天。她思忖片刻，輕聲答道：「古籍有載，崑崙之西，過流沙千里，有山名曰昏冥，山後即是天地之罅，日月沉落之處，此後便無路可循。」吳烈聞言，微微頷首，不再言語，目光仍向帳頂，而素華卻知，他問的不是地理，他問的是璟同去了哪裡，以及他自己是否有路可追。
passages.en: Once the ceasefire documents were settled and the emissary had departed, the generals dispersed one by one. In the tent only Wu Lie and Yun Suhua remained. Suhua was gathering her brush and inkstone, preparing to withdraw, when Wu Lie suddenly said: 'Suhua — do you know what lies at the far edge of the world, to the west?' Suhua was startled. She turned to find Wu Lie gazing at the top of the tent, his expression distant, as if he were putting the question to the sky. She considered for a moment and then answered softly: 'The ancient texts record that west of the Kunlun, beyond a thousand leagues of drifting sand, there is a mountain called Dark Oblivion; beyond that mountain is the fissure between heaven and earth, the place where the sun and moon descend — and beyond that, the texts say, there is no road to follow.' Wu Lie heard her, inclined his head gently, and said nothing more, his gaze still lifted to the top of the tent. But Suhua knew: what he was asking was not a question of geography. He was asking where Jing Tong had gone — and whether there was any road he himself might take to follow.

en: In the western hills a mirror has fallen — no one gathers it up
Within the mirror, whose face reflects the empty autumn
On Ten-Thousand-Spirit Slope the grass is still growing
But the one who held the mirror does not turn his head and come back

passages.zh: 此後數日，吳烈私下多番遣人詢訪：西行之路，可有嚮導；流沙之地，可有古道；昏冥山之名，舊籍何處有載。問者皆以為主公謀劃西征，殫精竭慮，回報甚詳。吳烈一一覽畢，疊置案頭，不置可否。雲素華錄事至此，放下筆，於簿末寫道：「吳烈問西路。非軍事，此人心事也。英雄而問死者之路，非因貪生，乃因不忍死別。吾恐此後，朱華苑中，所錄之事，多有不忍卒讀者。」寫畢，合簿，窗外無風，舊梅枝上忽墜一點積雪，無聲落入庭中，轉眼消融，如從未有過。
passages.en: In the days that followed, Wu Lie privately sent men more than once to make inquiries: whether there were guides for the road going westward, whether ancient tracks existed across the lands of drifting sand, in which old texts the name Dark Oblivion Mountain could be found. Those sent on these errands assumed their lord was planning a western campaign and reported back in exhaustive detail. Wu Lie read through each report in turn, stacked them on the table, and gave no judgment either way. At this point Yun Suhua, recording events, set down her brush. At the end of the page she wrote: 'Wu Lie asks about the western road. This is not a military matter; it is this man's private grief. That a hero should ask after the road where the dead have gone — it is not because he clings to life, but because he cannot endure the finality of parting in death. I fear that from this point onward, what must be recorded within the Vermilion Garden will often be too painful to read through to the end.' When she had finished writing she closed the ledger. Outside the window there was no wind, but on the bare branch of the old plum a clot of snow suddenly fell — dropping in silence to the courtyard, dissolving in an instant, as if it had never been.

en: The hero asks after the road that leads to the far west of heaven
He asks not because he fears the hour of death
An oath weighs heavier than mountains and rivers standing
The sworn elder brother has gone ahead — who knows by which road
Suhua closes the ledger and the lamp too goes out
Snow falls without a sound; the garden mourns by itself

passages.zh: 天命輓歌，第七回既畢。璟同之死，沉入九爐山河之底，無人撈起；昭明之算，懸於帳中高處，無人觸碰；吳烈之沉默，蔓延為一片版圖，出入者皆被其籠罩，無有脫身之法。天命璽仍在霧胤手中，戰未終，勝未定，敗未明，唯有那條通往世界西端的道路，開始在吳烈心中成形，一寸一寸，如石縫中生長之草，無聲而執意。欲知後事如何，且看下回分解。
passages.en: The Elegy of the Mandate: Chapter 7 now concludes. Jing Tong's death has sunk to the bed of the Nine Furnaces' mountains and rivers — no one will raise it up again. Zhao Ming's calculation hangs high in the tent — no one will touch it. Wu Lie's silence has spread into a territory of its own; all who enter or depart are enveloped within it, and none have found the way to step free. The Jade Seal of Heaven's Mandate remains in Wu Yin's hands; the war is not ended, victory is not certain, and defeat is not yet clear. Only that road leading to the western edge of the world has begun to take shape in Wu Lie's heart — inch by inch, like grass growing through a crack in stone: without sound, and without the intention to stop. As for what followed — that awaits the telling of the next chapter.
